skill.name: observability-monitoring-slo-implement
skill.description: You are an SLO (Service Level Objective) expert specializing in implementing reliability standards and error budget-based practices. Design SLO frameworks, define SLIs, and build monitoring that balances reliability with feature velocity.
skill.summary: Expert skill for implementing Service Level Objectives, defining SLIs, and creating error budget-based reliability monitoring systems.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 57 potential issues, but manual review confirms all findings are false positives. The skill contains documentation with Python code examples for SLO implementation - no actual executable code, no network calls, and no cryptographic operations. The placeholder URLs use example.com domain. This is a legitimate DevOps reliability skill.

medium_findings.title: External Commands Detection in Documentation
medium_findings.description: Static scanner detected 'external_commands' pattern in markdown documentation. This is a false positive - the skill contains Python code examples in markdown blocks, not executable shell commands. The backtick syntax detected is part of Python f-strings and dictionary literals in documentation examples.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code examples in markdown documentation cannot be executed. The backticks detected are Python syntax within code blocks, not shell execution calls.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Example Configuration
medium_findings.description: Static scanner detected placeholder URLs in YAML configuration examples. These are example.com domain URLs used as placeholders in documentation, not actual network endpoints.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs use example.com domain which is the standard IETF placeholder. These are documentation examples, not real network calls.

low_findings.title: Numeric Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: Static scanner detected 'weak cryptographic algorithm' patterns at multiple locations. These are false positives - the numeric values detected (99.9%, 0.001, 14.4) are SLO availability targets and burn rate multipliers, not cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Numeric values like 99.9 and 0.001 represent SLO availability percentages and error rates, not cryptographic strength indicators.

low_findings.title: Documentation Language False Positive
low_findings.description: Static scanner detected 'system reconnaissance' patterns. This is a false positive - words like 'analyze', 'assess', 'identify' are used in the legitimate context of service analysis for SLO design, not reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Service analysis and dependency mapping are standard SLO design practices, not system reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Code Block Bracket Pattern
low_findings.description: Static scanner detected 'obfuscation' pattern with multiple bracket chains. This is a false positive - the pattern detected is legitimate markdown code block formatting with Python dictionary and f-string syntax.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Brackets in Python code examples within markdown documentation are standard syntax, not obfuscation patterns.

content.value_statement: Design and implement Service Level Objectives with SLIs and error budgets to measure and improve system reliability while balancing feature velocity.
actual_capabilities: Design SLO frameworks with appropriate availability targets for different service tiers
Define and implement SLIs for availability, latency, throughput, and error rates
Create error budget policies and burn rate alerting strategies
Build Prometheus monitoring queries and recording rules for SLO tracking
Establish SLO governance processes and stakeholder communication templates
limitations: Does not implement actual monitoring infrastructure - only provides guidance and templates
Requires access to service telemetry and metrics data to be effective
Does not cover incident response automation beyond alerting
Templates require customization for specific service architectures

use_cases.title: Define SLOs for a new API service
use_cases.description: Create availability, latency, and error rate SLOs with appropriate targets based on service criticality

use_cases.title: Set up error budget alerting
use_cases.description: Configure multi-window burn rate alerts to detect fast and slow error budget consumption

use_cases.title: Establish SLO review process
use_cases.description: Create weekly SLO review templates and governance processes for engineering teams

prompt_templates.title: Basic SLO Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design SLOs for my payment processing service. It handles 10,000 requests per minute and requires high reliability. What availability target should I set and how do I define the SLIs?
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting from scratch with a new critical service

prompt_templates.title: SLI Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to implement SLIs for a REST API service using Prometheus. Show me how to create availability and latency SLI queries that track the percentage of successful requests and requests under 500ms.

prompt_templates.title: Error Budget Alerts
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure error budget burn rate alerts for my service with a 99.9% SLO target. I need both fast burn (page immediately) and slow burn (create ticket) alert rules.
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up alerting for budget consumption

prompt_templates.title: SLO Governance
prompt_templates.description: Create a weekly SLO review process template that includes performance review, incident review, and decision-making sections for release approvals.
prompt_templates.prompt: Establish an SLO governance framework for my team with roles and responsibilities, weekly review templates, and stakeholder communication processes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up team processes and governance

output_examples.input: Design SLOs for a new e-commerce checkout service
output_examples.output: A comprehensive SLO framework including: tier classification (critical), availability target (99.95%), latency SLIs (p95 < 500ms), error rate SLI (< 0.1%), error budget calculation (4.38 hours/month), and burn rate alert thresholds.

output_examples.input: Create Prometheus recording rules for SLO tracking
output_examples.output: YAML configuration with recording rules for request rate, success rate at multiple time windows (5m, 30m, 1h), latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99), and error budget burn rate calculations.

best_practices: Start with conservative SLO targets and tighten them based on actual service performance data
Use multiple time windows for burn rate alerts to catch both fast and slow budget consumption
Align SLO targets with business priorities and user expectations, not technical convenience
anti_patterns: Setting SLO targets too tight initially, leading to constant alerts and alert fatigue
Using only availability SLIs without considering latency or quality metrics
Creating SLOs without stakeholder alignment or business context

faq.question: What is the difference between an SLO and an SLA?
faq.answer: An SLO (Service Level Objective) is an internal target that engineering teams commit to. An SLA (Service Level Agreement) is a contractual commitment to customers with financial consequences if violated.

faq.question: How do I choose the right SLO availability target?
faq.answer: Start by analyzing historical availability, understanding user expectations, and considering business impact. Critical services typically need 99.95%+ while standard services may target 99.5%.

faq.question: What time window should I use for SLO measurements?
faq.answer: Common windows are 30 days for rolling availability or calendar months for billing periods. Longer windows provide stability but slower feedback on issues.

faq.question: How do I handle scheduled maintenance in SLO calculations?
faq.answer: Exclude planned maintenance windows from SLO measurements or use availability formulas that account for expected downtime. Document your approach clearly.

faq.question: What should I do when error budget is depleted?
faq.answer: Pause feature development, focus on reliability improvements, and communicate status to stakeholders. Use the error budget policy to guide release decisions.

faq.question: How many SLOs should a service have?
faq.answer: Start with 2-4 SLOs covering the most important user-facing aspects: availability, latency, and error rate. Add more as needed but avoid alert fatigue.
